I have just arrived. The flight has just landed.
I have already unpacked, and I have already checked in.
I haven't had dinner yet.
Have you checked in yet?
I have just arrived, and I am exhausted.
I have already checked in online.
So far, so good.
How has your trip been? So far, so good. I have seen three cities already.
You have had the facts since Lesson 2. Tonight you learn the three words that make them sound like you.
Good evening, and welcome to the Hotel Lisboa. You look tired, have you just arrived?
Yes, I have just arrived. The flight has just landed, and I am exhausted.
A long day. Have you checked in with us online, or shall I do it now?
I have already checked in online, this morning. My name is Rosangela Pinheiro de Oliveira.
Perfect, I have you here. Room 402, on the fourth floor. Good news, your room is ready. We have already cleaned it, and I have already put a spare key in the envelope for your husband.
Thank you. And the restaurant, is it still open? We haven't had dinner yet.
The kitchen has already closed, I am sorry. But I can send some soup and bread up to the room. It is midnight, and you have not eaten, you need something warm.
That is very kind. One more thing, could I have a wake-up call at eight? I don't trust my phone with the jet lag.
Of course. A wake-up call at eight, room 402. Tonight, do nothing. Drop off your bags, freshen up, and sleep. You can unpack and settle in tomorrow.
Tonight I have only one job, to sleep. Thank you, Tomas. You have made this very easy.
That is my job. Welcome to Lisbon, senhora. You have already done the hard part, you are here. Sleep well.
We haven't had dinner yet. Is the restaurant still open?
Could I have a wake-up call at eight, please?
So far, so good, I have already done the hard part.

Good evening, everybody, and welcome to the hotel. My name is Henrique, I am the night manager, and it is almost midnight, so I am going to be quick, because you have all had a very long day. Look at your faces. Some of you have just landed. Some of you have been on a plane for eleven hours. And some of you, I can see, have already fallen asleep standing up, and that is fine. So, three things, and then your keys. First: your rooms are ready. All of them. We have already cleaned them, we have already put out the water and the towels, and the beds are turned down. You do not have to do anything tonight. Drop off your bags, and go to sleep. Second: breakfast. It is downstairs, and it starts at seven and it finishes at ten. Now, listen to me. If you have just arrived from Brazil, or from Japan, or from anywhere far, you have not slept in your own bed for a day, and your body does not know what time it is. So do not put an alarm for six. Sleep. If you miss breakfast, we will find you a coffee. Nobody has ever died of a late breakfast in this hotel. Third, and this is the one people forget. If you want a wake-up call, call reception now, tonight, before you go up. Do not wait until the morning. Half of the people who miss their tour have not asked for the wake-up call yet. They think they will remember, and then jet lag wins. It always wins. So, have you asked for your wake-up call? No? The phone is right here. Now, your keys. And welcome to Lisbon. You have already done the hard part. You have arrived.

Hello. My name is Margarida, I am seventy-two, and I want to tell you about the first hour in a hotel, alone, because I think nobody tells you about that hour, and it is the hardest one. Not the airport. Not the plane. The first hour in the room. Here is what happens. You have just arrived. You are exhausted, you are jet-lagged, it is late, and you open the door of a room you have never seen, and it is silent, and it is not your home. And for about one minute, every single time, I want to go back. Seventy-two years old, nineteen countries, and I still want to go home for one minute in that doorway. So this is what I have learned to do, and I am going to give it to you, because it works. Do not sit down. If you sit down on that bed, jet-lagged, at midnight, it is over. Instead, do three small things, in the same order, every time. One: I drop off my bag and I open the curtains, even at night, so I can see where I am in the morning. Two: I unpack one thing. Not everything. One thing. Usually a photograph. And all of a sudden the room is a little bit mine. Three: I freshen up, I wash my face with cold water, I look in the mirror, and I say, out loud, in English, so it counts: I have arrived. I made it. And that is the hour. After that the room is yours, and the trip begins. I have done this in nineteen countries. My husband is not with me any more. He was the one who used to do the talking, and now it is me, and I am telling you: the language was never the hard part. The doorway was the hard part. And you have already walked through worse doorways than this one. Go. Open the curtains.

I didn't shower. I just freshened up.
It was an eleven-hour flight. I am exhausted.
We arrived on Monday, but we haven't checked out yet.
I have just arrived, and I am exhausted.
I have already checked in online.
We haven't had dinner yet. Is the restaurant still open?

So far, so good, I have already done the hard part.
